source.title: The Cerebellum: Adaptive Prediction for Movement and Cognition

confidence_rationale: This review provides a quantitative upper bound on cerebellar predictive capacity: up to approximately 1 second for skilled movement prediction. This directly supports MECH-231's core assertion that E2 operates as a short-horizon predictor. A sub-second biological bound maps to a small number of discrete steps in a grid-world environment, consistent with a 1-step transition predictor architecture. The paper also notes that temporal precision is a key distinguishing feature of cerebellar processing relative to cerebral cortex, reinforcing the E2 < E1 horizon ordering. Confidence is slightly higher than the 0.65 from the MECH-070 weakening entry because the quantitative bound now directly supports the corrected claim rather than opposing it.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Cerebellar predictive capabilities span relatively short time intervals relevant for skilled movement (up to a second or so); temporal precision is a key characteristic differentiating cerebellar processing from cerebral cortex; the cerebellum operates under tight temporal constraints.
mapping.ree_translation: E2's natural prediction horizon, as a cerebellar forward model analogue, is bounded to short intervals. A sub-second biological bound maps to a small number of discrete grid-world steps. MECH-231 claims E2 prediction accuracy degrades faster than E1's across multi-step horizons -- this is exactly what a system with a ~1-step native operating range would show when asked to roll out further. E1 (cortical, LSTM, longer-horizon) is not subject to the same temporal precision constraint.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 'up to a second or so' bound is in biological continuous time; mapping this to discrete REE steps depends on how one defines the temporal equivalent of a grid-world step, which is not formally specified. The paper also notes cerebellar involvement in sequencing and cognitive tasks beyond motor control, suggesting the temporal constraint may not apply uniformly across all cerebellar functions. These extended cognitive roles likely involve cerebro-cerebellar loops through cortex, which could yield longer effective horizons via cortical contributions -- but each cerebellar contribution within such loops would still be a short-horizon computation.
mapping.source_context: 2017 Trends in Cognitive Sciences review. PMC open access. Explicitly quantifies the cerebellar timescale constraint, which is rarer than qualitative characterisations and provides a testable biological anchor. Previously entered for MECH-070 with evidence_direction: weakens. Re-entered here with corrected direction for MECH-231.

confidence_components.notes: High quality source -- Trends in Cognitive Sciences, Popa et al. group has strong cerebellar electrophysiology background. Mapping fidelity slightly higher than in the MECH-070 entry (where it was 0.60) because the direction now aligns: the quantitative bound supports a short-horizon system, which is what MECH-231 claims. Transfer risk unchanged: the biological temporal constraint is on continuous prediction in biological time; REE's failure mode in discrete steps is different in kind, and the conversion factor is undefined.
